topic: Transitioning from procedural random walkers to Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) using classes and container classes to manage populations and color palettes.

concepts.name: Walker Class (The Individual)
concepts.explanation: A blueprint that defines what every individual walker knows (position, previous position, color) and does (update movement, display itself).
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to move from drawing one single line to creating hundreds of independent agents that all follow the same rules.

concepts.name: Walkers Class (The Container)
concepts.explanation: A manager class that holds an array of Walker objects and controls their collective properties, like a shared color palette.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to treat a group of objects as a single entity, making it easy to manage large populations and ensure visual cohesion.

concepts.name: Constructor
concepts.explanation: A special function within a class that runs once when you create a new instance (using 'new') to set up the object's initial state.
concepts.why_it_matters: It is essential for giving each new object its own unique starting position and properties.

concepts.name: Methods (update and display)
concepts.explanation: Functions that belong to a class. 'update' calculates the next position, and 'display' handles the actual drawing on the canvas.
concepts.why_it_matters: This encapsulates behavior within the object, so you only have to call 'walker.update()' instead of writing all the math every time.

concepts.name: Color Palettes via OOP
concepts.explanation: Instead of every walker picking a random color, the container class (Walkers) picks a base color and passes it to each individual walker.
concepts.why_it_matters: This creates 'color families' where objects look related rather than just being a chaotic mess of random colors.

concepts.name: Property Linking (Thickness to Step Size)
concepts.explanation: Tying one visual property to a movement property—for example, making thicker lines move more slowly by calculating step size based on thickness.
concepts.why_it_matters: It creates more organic, complex visual systems where appearance and behavior are interconnected.

code_examples.demonstrates: A basic procedural random walker using global variables.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to set px and py initially, which causes a line to draw from (0,0) to the first random point.

code_examples.demonstrates: Encapsulating the walker logic into a class.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to use 'this.' when referencing properties inside the class methods.

code_examples.demonstrates: Iterating through an array of objects to update and draw them all.
code_examples.common_issue: Trying to iterate over the class itself instead of the array stored inside the class.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The line doesn't draw correctly or jumps from the corner.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Not initializing 'px' and 'py' to the starting 'x' and 'y'.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Set `px = x` and `py = y` in the setup or constructor.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Trying to access a class property without using the 'this.' keyword (e.g., `x` instead of `this.x`).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Always use `this.` when referring to properties defined in the constructor.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'create').
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Trying to call a method on an object that hasn't been instantiated yet (calling `walkers.create()` before `walkers = new Walkers()`).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Ensure the object is instantiated with `new` before calling its methods.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Trying to use a `for...of` loop on the class instance itself rather than the array property inside it.
Trying to use `setRed()` on a value that isn't actually a p5 color object, or trying to use it as if it returns the color (it modifies in place).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use `red(this.stroke)` to get the value and then use `setRed()` correctly.

key_moments.description: Instructor uses code folding to show how complex logic can be simplified visually, helping manage cognitive load.
The instructor encounters a real error in the console and uses it to demonstrate how to debug 'undefined' errors.
The instructor explains how to link visual properties (thickness) to behavioral properties (step size) to create more organic movement.

leads_to: Advanced generative systems with complex agent interactions
student_questions: How do I make many lines move at once without writing code for each one?
Why is my code saying 'x is not defined' even though I created x in the constructor?
How do I make a group of objects all have similar colors?
What is the difference between a Walker and Walkers?
How do I make my shapes move faster or slower based on how big they are?
Why is my canvas blank even though I have code for a line?
How do I change the color of an object while it's moving?
What does 'this.' actually do in a class?
How do I use an array to keep track of many different objects?
Why am I getting a 'Cannot read properties of undefined' error?
